paragraphs.body: In my younger years, I was involved in a good number of different sports. In these activities we would weigh ourselves consistently, especially in wrestling and jiu-jitsu. The only thing I recall is stepping on the scale and needing to maintain a consistent weight in order to compete in wrestling. If you did not make the certain weight, they wanted you to be, you would not be allowed to wrestle. Being prohibited from wrestling would be extremely upsetting for both you and your team. At that age, the only memory I have is of gazing into the shiny mirror on the walls of the gym. Typically, people would feel sweaty and have a parched mouth after working out at the gym. As I kept grappling, I aged and went to high school. During this period, one can only know about how the weight classes differed from the lower grades. This time around, I needed to put on some pounds in order to compete against larger and more powerful opponents in wrestling. Attempting to fluctuate between losing and gaining weight during childhood is detrimental to both physical and mental health. While I was gaining weight, I made the decision to stop wrestling. Since they made it a priority to gain weight, I felt I did not want to do that anymore. As the year progressed, I began to lose interest in monitoring my weight. Soon I realized I was much heavier than I had anticipated because of my lack of carelessness. I can remember my teammates saying how I have gotten bigger and have passed my weight class. Them pointing this out made me really think if I liked to be bigger than I wanted to be in weight size. During my sophomore year of high school, I made the decision to prioritize my appearance and take more care in how I presented myself at school. I would constantly go on diets and go to the gym every day. I have been constantly checking the mirror since then, each time I walk by one, to see if I appear "fat" or bigger than usual. As the years went on, I can still remember my one friend group where all they cared about was your appearance. If you were not in their opinion “fit” it would be sad to hear that they did not like you. Close friends and boys I know always pick on each other about how they look. Why were boys so judgy out of everyone? I feel like going to high school know a day when you see others at the gym, you should respect one another and be proud of the other for getting out of bed and stepping foot in the gym. When I would hear people comment on others perspective of their size, it just did not stand right with me. Hearing the things and feeling like I was not happy with the way my weight had made me terribly upset.

paragraphs.body: The word upset can describe how a person feels and reacts to a situation. Being upset does not always have to manifest as a loud outburst of anger or an intense emotional breakdown. Feeling upset does not always have to mimic a raging storm swirling within you that is not coming to a stop. In the event of an upsetting situation, it may act as a gentle ache or a sense of irritation, rather than getting into a complete reaction of frustration. Getting upset is not just a reaction, it is much more than that. Those who feel upset are often the same individuals responsible for brewing the coffee every morning, getting through their days, feeding their families, doing schoolwork, and promptly returning phone calls. They continue their work regularly and remain receptive to the quiet voices that require their attention, even though they are in their own moments of distraction. Individuals feeling upset often try to keep their cool but may have to hide the feeling even more, resisting the urge to react spontaneously by slamming doors or screaming in frustration. The word upset can have a lot of different meanings to it, it just depends on how the person views it. Maybe even though the view of a mirror.

paragraphs.body: An object people use every day, which is a mirror. Mirrors are typically made of glass that has been gently coated in metal, such silver or aluminum, and have a smooth, reflective surface. The framing of the edges in a variety of materials, such as wood, metal, and plastic, may improve its aesthetic appeal. Sometimes mirrors can mean different things. It can make a person feel bad about themself or more confident in themselves. I can only remember when I was a kid always looking for reflection in the mirror and looking at car reflections, etc. Looking back at all the memories of wrestling and it all reminds me of the nasty smell of sweat. I remember not even going near the locker room because it would smell terrible but there was always a mirror in the room. The reason every man went into the room to take a picture of the shiny reflection on the wall. Constantly seeing boys on my team proud of their accomplishments at the gym and always feeling left out. Now it is a big trend to post one another “gym” progress on an Instagram account or a story. Doing this can make others either look up to the person or feel left out because it degrades a person's opinion on how they feel about their weight and size. I can remember the constant feeling of wanting to lose weight after viewing these posts. They really messed with me and frankly a lot of others I knew closely. I was constantly looking up meal plans and ways to quickly lose weight all because of what I saw in the mirror. The thing I always thought about was should you avoid going near the mirror at all because it could cause you pain physically or mentally. To me, a mirror means a lot of different things and can make me personally feel a certain way, when looking at one. I feel in my life a mirror resembles a lot about a person and can either help a person or make them upset. As I will always be proud of someone who shows how they look, and that feels confident. I just know some of the feelings of others around that would feel awful after seeing that someone can be so happy with their weight.

paragraphs.body: For these reasons, a mirror acts as a fascinating and unsettling object when examined closely, stirring a feeling of discomfort and self-reflection. Its reflective surface possesses the ability to disrupt one’s perception of reality, distorting self-image and questioning personal identity. What appears to be a common object, a mirror, transforms into a disturbed image that causes people to be upset, compelling us to face our authentic selves and the feelings we often seek to hide. The mirror’s capacity to both reflect and distort leaves us with an enduring sense of unease, a reminder that what we perceive may not always align with our desires.

annotations.text: The mirror’s capacity to both reflect and distort leaves us with an enduring sense of unease, a reminder that what we perceive may not always align with our desires.
